# Landing Page Content

## Decisions

- Primary reader: someone considering starting their first meetup or conference
- Core feeling: "this is possible for me"
- Tone: motivational, generative — no fragility/crisis framing
- Both guide tracks presented as live and equal

---

## Eyebrow

Living resources for organizers

## Headline

Ruby Community Guides

## Lede

Everything you need to start and run a Ruby community —
written by people who've done it.

## Body

You don't need a big budget, a large following, or years of event experience.
You need a room, a few willing people, and the willingness to keep showing up.

These guides were built from conversations with organizers around the world —
people who booked their first venue not knowing what they were doing,
cold-emailed their first speaker, and found out what actually matters
when you get a room full of Rubyists together.

> "There are rare times that you get a lot of people in a community together.
> It's a rare time for people to have face-to-face interaction and build health
> and deeper connection together. You need the technical content, because that's
> the affinity that draws us together. But that's not how you build relationships."
> — Jeremy Smith, Blue Ridge Ruby

> "I now know all these people from doing this I didn't know before.
> My circle has greatly expanded, and these are people I can reach out to
> and ask when I have a question, or help me with a job search or help them.
> It's really nice to have a community."
> — Spike Ilacqua, Rocky Mountain Ruby
